hook: A Bronze Age village hidden inside a collapsed limestone doline, invisible until you stand at the lip of the cave.
facts: The village sits inside a deep doline (collapse depression) in the heart of Mount Tiscali, on the boundary between the Supramonte di Oliena and Supramonte di Dorgali; it is invisible until you reach the inside of the cavity.
It comprises roughly 40 circular and rectangular dry-stone huts built and inhabited between approximately the 15th and 8th centuries BC.
The site was excavated seven times between 1994 and 2000 in the 'Operazione Nuraghe Mannu' project, with over 700 volunteers organised by the Superintendency.
Access is by a strenuous mountain hike only (no road); guided walks are offered from the Dorgali area.
The huts were roofed with wood and branches, using a building technique that differs from other Nuragic sites.
w: Hidden inside a collapsed mountain dome in the Supramonte, this Bronze Age village stays invisible until you scramble over the lip and find the huts huddled in the gloom of a vast cave. Sardinians sheltered here for millennia. No road reaches it; you earn the silence on foot. A lost city under a roof of stone.

hook: A Nuragic sacred well whose precision-cut stepped staircase descends to a tholos chamber over a spring of water.
facts: The sacred well is one of the best-preserved of the Nuragic civilisation, generally dated to around the 11th century BC.
It is composed of three precisely chiselled parts: a vestibule/atrium, a stepped keyhole-shaped stairwell, and a tholos (corbel-vaulted) hypogeal chamber over the water.
It lies within the Santa Cristina archaeological park, a Nuragic site of about one hectare that also includes a nuraghe, a meeting hut with enclosure, and other structures.
The monument is oriented roughly north-northwest to south-southeast.
An astronomical alignment is claimed: every 18.6 years, at the major lunar standstill, moonlight is said to fall through the ~30 cm roof opening onto the water below.
The park is open to visitors with paid entry and is part of the 2025 UNESCO-related prehistoric Sardinia recognition discussions.
legend: The adjacent rural sanctuary takes its name from Santa Cristina, and the site retains a Christian pilgrimage village (cumbessias) used for festival novenas.
w: Down a keyhole stair of dressed basalt, perfectly tapered, lies a Nuragic well so precisely built it still holds a mirror of water at the bottom. Some claim its shaft frames the moon at the 18-year lunar standstill and the sun at the equinox. Three thousand years on, the masonry stays flawless. Sacred water, engineered like a watch.

hook: The largest rock-cut 'fairy house' necropolis of pre-Nuragic Sardinia, found by accident in a winery's fields.
facts: It is the largest necropolis of pre-Nuragic Sardinia, consisting of 38 domus de janas (rock-cut chamber tombs).
It was discovered accidentally in 1903 during excavations for a farmhouse on the Sella & Mosca winery estate north of Alghero.
Archaeologist Antonio Taramelli carried out the first excavations in 1904, uncovering ten of the domus.
The tombs date to the Late Neolithic Ozieri culture (c. 3200-2800 BC) with continued use into the Copper and early Bronze Age (c. 2800-1600 BC).
Cultures attested at the site include Ozieri, Abealzu-Filigosa, Monte Claro, Bell Beaker and Bonnanaro.
The site was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2025 as part of the domus de janas serial nomination.
legend: Domus de janas means 'houses of the fairies/witches' in Sardinian; folklore holds the tiny rock chambers were homes of the janas, small supernatural women.
w: Sardinia's largest 'fairy house' necropolis hides in the flat farmland near Alghero: dozens of tombs cut into the rock five thousand years ago, carved with false doors, pillars and bull-horn reliefs. The dead were given homes, not holes. Local lore peopled them with the janas, tiny fairy-weavers. Step down into a Neolithic afterlife.

hook: A labyrinthine rock-cut necropolis whose 'Tomb of the Chief' has some 18 chambers and was later turned into a frescoed church.
facts: The complex comprises about twenty domus de janas carved into a trachyte ridge on the plain of Santa Lucia, a few kilometres from Bonorva.

Its largest tomb, the 'Tomba del Capo' (Tomb of the Chief), spans roughly 250 square metres and contains about 18 chambers arranged like a labyrinth around two main rooms, making it one of the largest hypogean tombs in the Mediterranean.
The site dates to the Ozieri culture of the Final Neolithic (c. 3500-2900 BC).
Some tombs were reused and structurally altered into the Middle Ages; an Early Christian fresco survives at the site, where a tomb was converted into a church.
It lies about 500 metres from the rural church of Santa Lucia.
It was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2025 within the domus de janas nomination.
legend: As with other domus de janas, local tradition attributes the rock-cut chambers to the janas, fairy-like beings of Sardinian folklore.
w: On a quiet plateau near Bonorva, the rock holds a warren of tombs cut six thousand years ago — among them the vast 'Tomb of the Chief,' with eighteen chambers shaped to mimic a house of the living. Early Christians later painted one into a frescoed church. Death, dwelling and faith, layered in the same stone. Bring a torch.

hook: Dubbed the 'cathedral of the domus de janas' — a tomb the size of a three-storey house carved into a boulder in the middle of the village.
facts: La Rocca is carved entirely into a huge rock outcrop on the edge of the Baldana valley in the centre of Sedini, and is regarded as perhaps the largest domus de janas on the island ('the cathedral of the domus de janas').
It dates to the 4th-3rd millennium BC and originally held several hypogeal chambers, around 2.5 m long and 1.5 m wide.
Unlike most isolated Neolithic tombs, it stands within the urban fabric and has served many later functions: prison, stable, shop, dwelling and meeting place.
The interior preserves a central hearth and a spiral staircase carved into the rock.
In the 1990s the municipality acquired it and turned it into a permanent ethnographic museum.
It is part of the 2025 UNESCO domus de janas recognition.
legend: Sedini lore links the chambers to the janas (fairies); the village is known locally for its domus de janas and fairy traditions.
w: A craggy boulder in the middle of Sedini, hollowed five thousand years ago for the dead and reused ever since — as a medieval prison, then homes, now a little ethnographic museum. Locals call it the cathedral of the fairy houses for its sheer scale. Few tombs have been lived in this long. The village simply grew around its bones.

hook: A wind-sculpted trachyte boulder that looks like a crouching elephant, with prehistoric tombs carved into its body.
facts: The roughly 4-metre-high trachyte rock, shaped by erosion into an elephant-like form, stands at km 4.3 of the SS134 road between Castelsardo and Sedini, in the locality of Multeddu.
Two domus de janas are carved into the rock on two levels: the upper Tomb I is badly weathered, while the lower Tomb II is very well preserved with a dromos, anticella and multiple cells.
The tombs are pre-Nuragic, dated by their carved details to the first half of the 3rd millennium BC (recent Neolithic, c. 3300-2500 BC).
Tomb II features carved bull-horn (bucrania) protomes associated with the funerary cult.
The site was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2025 under the domus de janas nomination.
It sits beside the public road and is freely accessible at the trailhead/roadside.
legend: The domus de janas inside are tied to the fairy (janas) folklore common across Sardinia's rock-cut tombs.
w: Wind and rain have whittled a roadside lump of trachyte into the unmistakable shape of an elephant, trunk and all, on the road below Castelsardo. Long before it looked like one, Neolithic hands cut tombs into its flank — fairy houses inside the beast. Geology and grief, in one weathered grey monument. You'll spot it from the car.

hook: Sardinia's densest field of standing stones — some sixty menhirs, megalithic tombs and a domus de janas ringed by stone circles.
facts: Pranu Muttedu is one of the most important pre-Nuragic funerary areas in Sardinia and has the island's highest known concentration of menhirs (about sixty), variously set in pairs, groups and rows.
One notable alignment runs 18 sandstone monoliths along an east-west axis over roughly 32 metres.
The site also includes two megalithic tombs and a domus de janas surrounded by stone circles.
It was systematically investigated between 1975 and 1984 under archaeologist Enrico Atzeni.
The complex is associated with the Final Neolithic Ozieri culture (4th-3rd millennium BC).
It is a managed open-air archaeological park near Goni with paid/guided visitor access.
w: A wild plateau near Goni studded with standing stones — some sixty menhirs, a few aligned toward the rising sun, scattered among rock-cut tombs. Five and a half thousand years old, it ranks as the densest menhir field on the island. Cork oaks throw shade over the silent ranks. Sardinia's own answer to Stonehenge, and far emptier.

hook: Over forty rock-cut tombs ranged across a natural trachyte amphitheatre on the flank of a 'silent' hill.
facts: The necropolis has about forty domus de janas arranged scenographically across a natural trachyte amphitheatre on the southern flank of the hill of Sa Pranedda, a few kilometres north of Villaperuccio in the Sulcis.
It is considered one of the largest and most important pre-Nuragic archaeological sites in Sardinia.
It was in use for roughly a millennium and a half, from the Final Neolithic (c. 3200-2800 BC) into the early Bronze Age (c. 1800-1600 BC).
Two notable tomb-sanctuaries, Sa Cresiedda ('little church') and Sa Grutta de is Procus ('pig cave'), have semicircular megalithic alignments and monumental entrances about two metres high.
Several chambers preserve carved decoration including spirals, concentric motifs and bull-horn (bucrania) symbols.
The site was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2025 within the domus de janas nomination and is an open archaeological park.
legend: The tombs are known as domus de janas, 'houses of the fairies', from the Sardinian legend of the janas who were said to inhabit such rock-cut chambers.
w: In a natural amphitheatre of rock above Villaperuccio, forty fairy-house tombs honeycomb the cliffs, a few carved with spirals and the staring 'eyes' of a Neolithic goddess. The Sulcis hills wrap the whole necropolis in wild quiet. Climb between the chambers and feel watched. The dead got the best view in the valley.

hook: One of Sardinia's best-preserved nuraghi: a complex basalt tower-fortress on the Abbasanta plateau.
facts: A complex (polylobed) nuraghe with a central keep and a triangular three-towered bastion, set on the basaltic plateau of Abbasanta at about 304 m above sea level, in the province of Oristano.
The central bastion dates to the last centuries of the 2nd millennium BC; the surrounding antemural and outer enclosure wall were built between roughly the 9th and 8th centuries BC.
It was the first nuraghe in Sardinia to be systematically investigated for archaeological research and valorisation purposes.
The surrounding settlement and walls enclose a wider area, and excavations recovered pottery, stone tools and bronze objects.
It lies a short distance from the SS131 trunk road (around km 123-124) between Abbasanta and Paulilatino, and is open as a managed visitor site.
It is included on Italy's UNESCO World Heritage Tentative List entry for the Nuragic monuments of Sardinia.
w: A colossus of black basalt rising from the Abbasanta tableland, its central tower still standing after three and a half thousand years. One of the best-preserved nuraghi on an island with seven thousand of them. Climb the inner stair into the corbelled dark. Bronze Age engineering that simply refused to fall.

hook: A well-preserved Nuragic village of round huts with two megaron temples, hidden in olive groves near Dorgali.
facts: A Nuragic settlement of roughly a hundred circular stone huts, dating from the 2nd millennium BC, in the municipality of Dorgali, province of Nuoro.
The village includes two sacred enclosures each containing a megaron-type temple (in antis), with vestibules and stone benches along the walls, probably linked to a water cult.
The huts had stone bases and were originally roofed with branches; many open onto a shared courtyard with a well.
It was excavated by Doro Levi between 1936 and 1938, and restored in 1961 under Guglielmo Maetzke.
It is a managed, ticketed visitor site reached from the Dorgali-Cala Gonone area and is part of the local archaeological circuit.
It is included in Italy's UNESCO Tentative List proposal for the Nuragic monuments of Sardinia.
w: A whole Bronze Age village frozen near Dorgali: a hundred or so round stone huts clustered around two little megaron temples and open courtyards. Walk the lanes and the Nuragic everyday comes close — hearths, wells, the shapes of doorways. Few sites show the ordinary life behind the famous towers. A small town, three thousand years dead.

hook: A towering multi-tower nuraghe crowning a schist plateau in the mountainous heart of Barbagia.
facts: A complex nuraghe with a central keep and a quadrilobate bastion of four corner towers, the largest archaeological site in the Barbagia di Belvi, in Meana Sardo territory, province of Nuoro.
It stands on Cuccuru Nolza, the highest point of the schist plateau of Su Pranu, at roughly 739-740 m above sea level.
The central tower survives to about 12-13 m in height, and a hut village spreads over about 2.5 hectares around it.
The towers were built in differing techniques, using shale blocks in a sub-filaric arrangement and porphyry blocks in a polygonal technique.
It was described by Vittorio Angius in 1842 and investigated through several excavation campaigns beginning in 1994.
It is managed as a visitor site (by the local Coop. Ortuabis) about 7-8 km from the village of Meana Sardo.
w: High on a windswept schist plateau above Meana Sardo, this many-towered nuraghe commands the whole Barbagia — valleys folding away in every direction. The climb keeps the crowds off; often you'll have its ramparts to yourself. Built to watch and to warn, it still does the job. A Bronze Age fortress with the island's best balcony.

hook: The Sinis-peninsula field where the giant Nuragic stone warriors, archers and boxers were unearthed.
facts: The find-site of the Giants of Mont'e Prama, larger-than-life Nuragic stone statues of warriors, archers and boxers standing roughly 2 to 2.5 m tall, on farmland in the comune of Cabras, province of Oristano.
The statues and associated tombs were discovered by chance in March 1974 on the Sinis Peninsula, north of the Gulf of Oristano near the Cabras lagoon.
Excavation campaigns ran between 1975 and 1979, recovering roughly 5,000 fragments including about fifteen heads and twenty-two torsos.
Fieldwork resumed from 2014 under the 'Archeologia del territorio di Mont'e Prama' project of the Universities of Sassari and Cagliari, uncovering further statues and a row of tombs.

The reassembled statues are displayed in the Giovanni Marongiu Civic Museum in Cabras and in the National Archaeological Museum of Cagliari, while the excavation area itself can be visited on the peninsula.
The statues are usually dated to around the Early Iron Age (roughly 9th-8th centuries BC), making them among the oldest large-scale anthropomorphic statues in the Mediterranean.
legend: Local tradition speaks of an ancient race of 'giants' that built Sardinia's prehistoric monuments, a folk motif echoed in the name commonly given to these statues.
w: In a flat Sinis field near Cabras, a farmer's plough struck stone in 1974 and turned up an army of giants: sandstone warriors, archers and boxers, smashed and buried over two thousand years ago. The reassembled colossi now stand in Cabras; the haunted ground where they slept is here. The oldest monumental statues in the Mediterranean.

n: Tomba dei Giganti di Coddu Vecchiu
hook: A Giants' Tomb whose 4-metre arched granite stele is the tallest known in Sardinia.
facts: A Bronze Age Giants' Tomb (collective burial monument) in the Capichera locality of Arzachena, province of Sassari, in the Gallura region of northern Sardinia.
Its central stele, formed of two stacked granite slabs, stands about 4.04 m high, the tallest such stele known in Sardinia, with a small opening at its base for offerings.

It began as a gallery tomb around the 18th century BC and was reworked into a Giants' Tomb in the Middle Bronze Age (roughly 16th-14th centuries BC) with the addition of the curved exedra and monumental stele.
The funerary gallery (burial corridor) behind the stele is about 10.5 m long.
It lies about 700 m from the La Prisgiona nuraghe and is a ticketed, managed visitor site run by GE.SE.CO. Arzachena.
It is roughly 2 km from the separate Li Lolghi Giants' Tomb, also near Arzachena.
legend: The name 'tomba dei giganti' comes from Sardinian folk belief that such oversized monuments were the graves of an ancient race of giants.
w: A great curved forecourt of upright slabs, centred on a carved stele nearly four metres tall — the gateway to a long Bronze Age burial gallery near Arzachena. The Nuragic dead were laid in the chamber behind; the living gathered at the crescent to mourn. Sardinians called such tombs the graves of giants. Stoop through and see why.

n: Tomba dei Giganti di Li Lolghi
hook: A long Giants' Tomb with a sweeping slab exedra and a tall arched central stele.
facts: A Bronze Age Giants' Tomb in the Li Muri area of Arzachena, province of Sassari, in the Gallura region of northern Sardinia.
Its facade is a large exedra of 14 upright slabs rising toward the centre, where a carefully worked arched stele about 3.75 m high stands, with a small door at its base.
The whole sepulchre is about 27 m long, making it one of the largest and most celebrated Giants' Tombs on the island.
It stands roughly 200 m from the Neolithic stone-circle necropolis of Li Muri, illustrating long-running prehistoric use of the area.
Like Coddu Vecchiu, it began as an earlier gallery tomb later enlarged into a Giants' Tomb in the Bronze Age.
It is a managed, ticketed visitor site near Arzachena, about 2 km from Coddu Vecchiu.
legend: As with other 'tombe dei giganti', the name reflects the Sardinian legend that giants were buried in these monuments.
w: A twin to nearby Coddu Vecchiu, this Giants' Tomb spreads its semicircle of stones along a low Gallura ridge, a tall portal-stele guarding the corridor behind. Begun as a single grave, it grew into the long galleried tomb you walk today. Sunset rakes the exedra and the old stones glow. The clan's dead, gathered under giants' names.

n: Tomba dei Giganti di S'Ena 'e Thomes
hook: An isolated, remarkably intact Giants' Tomb with a near-4-metre granite stele on the road to Lula.
facts: A well-preserved Bronze Age Giants' Tomb in the territory of Dorgali, province of Nuoro, dated to the Early Bronze Age (roughly 1800-1600 BC).
Its central granite stele is nearly 4 m high with smooth edges and weighs about seven tons, set at the centre of a horn-shaped exedra of stone slabs.
Behind the stele runs an almost intact funerary corridor about 11 m long, roofed with horizontal stone slabs.
Unusually for Sardinian Giants' Tombs, whose exedras typically face southeast, its exedra is oriented to the south, possibly linked to the summer-solstice sunset.
It stands isolated in open country about 6 km from the Serra Orrios village and roughly 17 km from Dorgali town, along the road toward Lula, and is freely accessible.
It is one of the most photographed and best-preserved Giants' Tombs in Sardinia.
legend: Its name 'tomba dei giganti' stems from the Sardinian folk tradition that such monuments were the tombs of giants.
w: Alone in a green plain under the Dorgali mountains stands one of the most perfect Giants' Tombs on the island: a single huge portal-stone, split by a small low door, fronting the slab-roofed burial gallery. No ticket office, no crowds, just the wind and the grazing sheep. Walk up and put your hand on three thousand years.

hook: A megalithic Nuragic burial gallery with a broken arched stele, hidden on a hillside in the sacred Sarcidano uplands near Nurallao.
facts: A 'tomba dei giganti' (giants' tomb), the collective megalithic burials of Sardinia's Bronze Age Nuragic civilisation.
Sits on the eastern slope of a hill about 3 km from Nurallao, in an area used for ritual/funerary purposes since pre-Nuragic times.
The tomb was discovered during agricultural work; its entrance faces east, preceded by a semicircular exedra of orthostatic limestone slabs roughly 6 m wide.
At the centre of the exedra stand the remains of a broken arch-shaped stele, at whose base a rectangular doorway opens.
The funerary chamber is about 10 m long, preceded by a narrow corridor lined with orthostatic slabs.
legend: Such tombs are called 'tombe dei giganti' in Sardinian folklore because local tradition held that only giants could have moved and assembled the huge stones.
w: Out in the Sarcidano hills near Nurallao, a low line of megaliths marks one of the lesser-walked Giants' Tombs, its burial corridor still traceable among the stones and scrub. Few signs point the way, which is rather the appeal. The clan that built it left no name, only the long grave and the giants' legend. You may well be alone with it.

hook: A cluster of four giants' tombs on a plateau in the Madau valley, high on the slopes of Gennargentu below the Corr'e Boi pass.
facts: The Madau necropolis comprises four similar giants' tombs rising on a plateau in the Madau river valley, at the foot of the Corr'e Boi pass.
It lies near the frazione of Pratobello, about 15 km from Fonni, the highest town in Sardinia, on the slopes of Gennargentu.
The burials date to the recent or final Bronze Age and may be connected to the nearby Nuragic village of Gremanu.
The site was excavated and studied between 1982 and 1986 by Giovanni Lilliu, considered the 'father' of Sardinian archaeology.
The tombs are giants' tombs (tombe dei giganti), the collective megalithic burial monuments of the Nuragic civilisation.
legend: The monuments are popularly called 'tombe dei giganti' from the folk belief that giants were buried in, or built, these oversized stone graves.
w: Tucked into forest on the Gennargentu slopes above Fonni sit a cluster of four Giants' Tombs, their stone exedrae scattered among the trees. At over a thousand metres, this counts as some of the wildest archaeology in Sardinia. Hike up through the oaks and find the dead waiting in the green shade. Barbagia at its most haunting.

hook: A Nuragic giants' tomb discovered in the 1990s, set among holm-oak woodland in the Osono locality near Triei in Ogliastra.
facts: A tomba dei giganti, a collective megalithic burial of the Nuragic age, in the Osono locality near Triei in the Ogliastra region.
It was discovered in the early 1990s and is recorded as a distinct archaeological site (Wikidata Q3992604).
The monument follows the classic giants'-tomb layout, with a burial corridor and a frontal exedra.
It stands in a forested setting on the eastern side of Sardinia, in the mountainous Ogliastra interior.
legend: Sardinian tradition attributes such oversized stone graves to giants, giving the monument type its name 'tomba dei giganti'.
w: Deep in the wooded hills above Triei, this Ogliastra Giants' Tomb hides at the end of a little-trodden path, its facade and burial gallery half-swallowed by holm oaks. Reaching it is part of the ritual — the old funerary routes ran through country like this. Quiet, green and entirely yours. The giants buried their dead well off the map.

hook: A giants' tomb famed for its towering carved central stele, one of the finest such monuments known in Sardinia, near Borore.
facts: A tomba dei giganti in the territory of Borore in the Marghine region, dating to the Middle Bronze Age.
Its centrepiece is a monolithic carved stele about 3.65 m tall, with an upper lunette measuring around 1.21 m at the centre.
The site was first identified in 1840 by cartographer Alberto La Marmora during his 'Voyage en Sardaigne'.
Scottish archaeologist Duncan Mackenzie photographed and described it in the early 20th century.

Giovanni Pinza, in his 1901 work on primitive Sardinian monuments, called it 'the finest sepolcro of giants known in Sardegna'; the central stele and parts of the exedra survive, while the burial chamber has been lost.
legend: The name 'tomba dei giganti' reflects the folk belief that only giants could have raised and shaped the monument's massive stones.
w: A single field near Borore holds one of the loveliest Giants' Tombs in central Sardinia: a rounded central stele, finely dressed and framed by a low door, standing where the burial gallery has all but gone. The Marghine plain rolls away around it, big and empty. Come at golden hour for the stone alone. Pure Nuragic atmosphere, no fuss.

n: Sa Domu 'e s'Orcu Tomba dei Giganti (Siddi)
hook: One of Sardinia's best-preserved giants' tombs, 'the House of the Ogre', built of finely worked basalt overlooking the Giara di Siddi.
facts: A tomba dei giganti at Siddi in Marmilla, recorded in Wikidata as Q1801594 and one of the best-preserved Nuragic monuments of its type.
Its name 'Sa Domu 'e s'Orcu' means 'the House of the Ogre' in Sardinian.
It is dated to the Middle Bronze Age (roughly 1500-1300 BC).
The burial chamber is about 15.20 m long, oriented along a southeast-northwest axis, with a frontal exedra around 18 m wide.
It is built of large basalt blocks quarried from the plateau on which it stands, finely sculpted throughout, and overlooks the Giara di Siddi.
legend: The Sardinian name attributes the tomb to 's'Orcu', an ogre; like other giants' tombs, local tradition links the oversized stones to giant or ogre builders rather than human ones.
w: Its name means 'the ogre's house,' and the Giants' Tomb on the basalt plateau above Siddi has the bulk to earn it — a long megalithic gallery roofed with massive slabs, perched on the wild Giara. Sardinians blamed such graves on man-eating giants and orcs. Climb the plateau for the tomb and the views both. Folklore built into the rock.

hook: A 15 m limestone sea-arch on Sardinia's west coast, the surviving vault of a collapsed coastal cave and a celebrated cliff-diving spot.
facts: S'Archittu di Santa Caterina is a protected natural monument in the frazione of S'Archittu, municipality of Cuglieri, province of Oristano.
The arch is a rocky bridge over the sea about 15 m high, the residual vault of an ancient coastal cave hollowed out by marine erosion.
It is formed of marine limestone of the middle Miocene, roughly 15-11 million years old.
The seabed beneath the arch reaches about 9 m in depth, and a paved path of roughly 500 m gives access on foot.
At about 15 m high it hosted a high-diving world championship event in 2001, and cliff-diving from its summit remains popular.
The site takes its name from the nearby Church of Santa Caterina, a relic of the old village of Pittinuri.
w: A pale limestone arch leaps clean across a turquoise cove on the west coast, the roof of a sea cave that finally collapsed. Brave swimmers clamber up and dive from the top into the deep water below, a local rite of summer. The little resort of Santa Caterina grew up around the spectacle. Nature's bridge, with a high board attached.

hook: A flood-condemned ghost village on the slopes of Monte Trunconi, emptied after 1951 and finally abandoned in the 1960s.
facts: Sits on the right slope of Monte Trunconi above the left bank of the Rio Pardu, at roughly 520 m above sea level.
A violent storm and five days of rain in October 1951 triggered landslides that made the centuries-old village (built on unstable, slipping ground) unsafe.
Its name 'gairo' is glossed as 'land that flows', reflecting the chronic subsidence that plagued it from the late 19th century.
The last inhabitants left around 1963, after which the population resettled at Gairo Sant'Elena, Taquisara and Cardedu.
Today it survives as a roofless open-air ruin and is one of Ogliastra's best-known abandoned villages, visited as a heritage site.
w: Evacuated after catastrophic floods in 1951 and finally abandoned in the 1960s, this Ogliastra village still stands roofless on its damp slope, slogans sprayed across the empty houses. The wet that doomed it lingers as valley fog. Slowly the macchia takes the streets back. A whole town walked away and left its stone shell to the weather.

hook: A near-deserted medieval hamlet on a rocky spur above the Santa Lucia plain, bound by a curse that it would never exceed thirty houses.
facts: Sits isolated on a rocky outcrop of the Meilogu, about 6 km from Bonorva, at roughly 408 m above sea level.
It was once the capital of the curatoria of Costavalle, in a strategic border position between the Giudicati of Arborea and Torres.
After a medieval heyday its population collapsed, with malaria cited as a major driver of depopulation.
The hamlet was later partly repopulated but remained tiny, and is today a near-ghost village restored in part for art and hospitality use.
legend: Legend says Donoria, daughter of the feudal lord King Beccu, was accused of witchcraft and exiled as her house burned; departing, she cursed the village - 'Rebeccu, Rebecchei, da 'e trinta domos non movei' ('Rebeccu will never pass thirty houses').
Mindful of the curse, later inhabitants are said to have stopped building at thirty houses so as not to test Donoria's prophecy.
w: A near-empty medieval hamlet above Bonorva, down to a handful of souls and tied to a curse: the princess Donoria, banished for witchcraft, is said to have damned Rebeccu never to hold more than thirty houses. It never has. Stone lanes, a Romanesque church and an uncanny stillness. Whether curse or hard luck, the village stopped growing.

hook: A tiny near-ghost medieval village minutes from Nuoro, said to be cursed never to grow and never to die.
facts: A small stone-built medieval hamlet about ten minutes from Nuoro, known for its narrow cobbled alleys and well-preserved vernacular architecture.
It peaked at roughly 540 inhabitants in the 1950s but has since dwindled to around a dozen permanent residents (about 12 as of 2022).
Grazia Deledda set her 1920 novel 'La madre' in the village, drawing on its convent and atmosphere.
It is frequently cited as one of Sardinia's last nearly abandoned medieval villages.
legend: Legend holds that Franciscan 'penitent' nuns from the village convent were accused of relations with local shepherds; shamed and expelled, they cursed Lollove - that, like the waters of the sea, it would never again grow nor appear to grow.
w: A cluster of stone houses in a fold of hills near Nuoro, Lollove counts barely a dozen residents and a famous curse: expelled nuns, the story goes, doomed it to be like sea water, never to grow and never to die. Centuries on, it has done exactly that. A ruined convent, and a silence you can hear. Frozen by legend, or just by isolation.

hook: An abandoned company mining village on Monte San Giovanni, built in the 1870s and left to ruin when the pits closed.
facts: Lies on a plateau at about 240 m on Monte San Giovanni, beside the Seddas Moddizzis mine, in the municipality of Gonnesa in the Iglesias mining district.
Engineer and politician Giorgio Asproni had the eponymous village built in 1874 to house the workforce of the surrounding lead-zinc mines.
At its height it held roughly 300 people and had a church dedicated to San Giorgio, a company store, management offices, the engineer's villa and a school.
It was abandoned in the mid-1970s as mining ceased and activity shifted toward Iglesias, and now survives as an industrial-archaeology ghost village visited on guided tours.
w: A roofless mining village in the Iglesiente, built for the Seddas Moddizzis pits and emptied when the ore ran out. Now houses, school and offices stand gutted among the maquis. Miners whispered of a headless rider, the ghost of a hated boss, patrolling the lanes after dark. Industrial Sardinia, returned to the scrub and the stories.

hook: The skeletal ruin of a vast seafront ore-washing plant on the Costa Verde, built to treat zinc blende from the Ingurtosu mines.
facts: Stands at Naracauli, beyond Ingurtosu along the SP66/SP4 in the municipality of Arbus, near the Piscinas dunes on the Costa Verde.
Inaugurated in 1900 and named after the English mine owner Lord Thomas Allnutt Brassey, it processed 'blenda' (sphalerite), the ore from which zinc is extracted.
It treated mineral from the rich Brassey lode, a continuation of the Montevecchio mineralisation worked from the Ingurtosu mine.
It operated intermittently until the end of the 1960s and now stands as a roofless skeleton, part of the UNESCO-affiliated Geomineral Park of Sardinia.
w: On the wild Costa Verde, gaunt arches of an ore-washing plant rise from the dunes, where the Ingurtosu mine once washed lead and zinc beside the sea. Named for an English shareholder, the Laveria Brassey worked into the last century, then fell to the salt wind. Grazia Deledda set a novel in these hills. Industry as romantic ruin.

hook: A ruined 13th-century hilltop castle on a volcanic plug near Siliqua, tied to Dante's Count Ugolino della Gherardesca.
facts: Perched on a steep volcanic hill rising from the Cixerri plain near Siliqua, roughly 30 km west of Cagliari, at around 256 m above sea level.
It is traditionally attributed to the Pisan nobleman Ugolino della Gherardesca, Count of Donoratico, from about 1257, after he became lord of south-western Sardinia.
Ugolino is the figure damned in Dante's Inferno (Cantos XXXII-XXXIII), starved to death in a Pisan tower.
The hilltop preserves the ruins of walls, towers and cisterns and is today an archaeological site open to visitors.
w: A ruined castle crowns a volcanic plug above the Siliqua plain, once held by Count Ugolino della Gherardesca — the Pisan lord Dante damned to the ice of the Inferno for treachery, gnawing his enemy's skull. Climb the cone for the broken walls and a view across half the south. Dante's villain owned this rock. The poetry came later.

hook: A rationalist 'citta di fondazione' raised from nothing in 16 months to mine coal for Mussolini's autarky, inaugurated in 1938.
facts: Built in 1937-38 as a coal-mining 'new town' to support Mussolini's policy of economic self-sufficiency (autarky); its first stone was laid on 9 June 1937.
It was officially inaugurated on 18 December 1938 at a ceremony attended by Benito Mussolini in person.
Its urban plan radiates from the Grande Miniera di Serbariu coal mine, with the three main axes converging on the pithead.
The civic centre, Piazza Roma, is framed by rationalist landmarks including the town hall, theatre, the Torre Littoria and the church of San Ponziano.
It is a recognised site of 20th-century rationalist architecture and is part of the ATRIUM route of totalitarian-era urbanism.
w: Conjured from bare ground in under two years and inaugurated in 1938, Mussolini's coal city stands as rationalism made concrete: a vast geometric piazza, a blunt fascist tower, a church and a cinema, all for the miners of Sulcis. Built for autarky and Italian coal, it outlived the ideology that drew it. Politics, still in the stone.

hook: A former 'devil's island' prison off Sardinia's north-west tip, now a national park - this pin marks the Cala Reale anchorage.
facts: Asinara lies at the north-western tip of Sardinia, administratively part of the municipality of Porto Torres in the Province of Sassari.
Nicknamed 'Isola del Diavolo', it served as a quarantine station, a WWI prisoner-of-war camp, and one of Italy's highest-security prisons during the anti-terrorism and anti-mafia years.
At Cala Reale a lazaretto (the Primario Lazzaretto del Regno d'Italia) was built in 1885; the bay holds a royal palace and a sea-turtle rescue centre (CRAMA).
It became the Asinara National Park in 1997 and is known for its wild albino donkeys and protected marine area.
w: A long island off the north-west tip, sealed off for a century — first as a quarantine station, then a maximum-security prison that held Mafia bosses. Locked away from development, its beaches and macchia turned into a pristine national park, roamed now by a herd of small white albino donkeys. Captivity, then accidental paradise.

hook: A sheer limestone island off Olbia styled 'the world's smallest kingdom' - this pin marks the Spalmatore di Terra settlement.
facts: A steep limestone island off the north-east coast of Sardinia, off Porto San Paolo, in the territory of Olbia.
Spalmatore di Terra, at the island's south-west end, is the landing point, with a small harbour, beach, eateries and a scattering of houses.
In 1836 King Charles Albert of Savoy is said to have named Paolo Bertoleoni 'King of Tavolara', the origin of its 'smallest kingdom in the world' fame.
The island's heights carry a long-range VLF naval radio transmitter, with a tall mast at the north-eastern Spalmatore di Fuori.
legend: The island's lore tells that King Charles Albert came to hunt Tavolara's wild goats - whose teeth were stained gold by a local plant - and rewarded the resident Bertoleoni family by crowning Paolo king of the tiny realm.
w: A sheer limestone slab heaving from the sea off Porto San Paolo and, by local reckoning, the world's smallest kingdom. The story runs that a passing king crowned the Bertoleoni rulers of Tavolara in the 1830s, a title islanders still wear with a wink. Hike its ridge, eat at the family restaurant below. A mountain, an island, a monarchy.
